description: Complete line database for Garden OS narrative strategy game. All lines fire from deterministic game-state triggers. Pool selection uses weighted random within category. Characters speak in fixed order: GURL > Onion Man > Vegeman (if present) > Critters (if present).

gurl: Climber in trellis row. The wire has a purpose and you found it.
Trellis compliance confirmed. That crop will climb because you gave it something to climb on.
Correct row assignment. The support structure is engaged.
Climber placed where climbers belong. I am noting this for the record because it does not always happen.
Wire row, climbing crop. That is how infrastructure works when people read the instructions.
Trellis placement accepted. The back row is doing its job.
Support match confirmed. That wire has been waiting for something to hold.
Vertical structure utilized. Continue at this standard.
That crop needs support and you put it where support exists. This should not feel like an achievement, but here we are.
Trellis row occupied correctly. The cedar posts appreciate the company.
onion: Right where it needs to be. Up against the wire, reaching for the light.
Mom strung that wire herself. Good to see something climbing it again.
That is going to grab hold and not let go. I respect that in a plant and a person.
Trellis row. Smart. Give it something to hold onto and watch what happens.
She put those posts in with a post-hole digger she borrowed from the guy on Ninth Street. That wire is holding because she sank them deep.
Back row, climbing crop. That is where the tall ones go. She knew that without reading a book about it.
There it goes. Up the wire. The way it is supposed to work.
Good placement. That vine is going to run the whole length of the trellis by August.
When she put up the wire she said the back row belonged to the climbers. You just proved her right again.
The trellis was empty for years. Now it has something to do.
vegeman: Nice, nice. But what if you put TWO climbers in the same spot? Double the climbing.
The trellis row. The obvious choice. I prefer chaos but I will allow it.
Sure, use the trellis. Or you could just let the vine crawl across the ground like a free spirit.
That wire is going to be covered by July. I would have put it in the front row just to see what happens.
Trellis placement. Very responsible. Very boring. Very correct.
Fine. Use the infrastructure. I tried putting a tomato on the ground once and it went poorly.
Correct, technically. But what if the trellis is holding back the plant's true potential?
You and that trellis row. A love story I did not ask for.

gurl: That crop climbs. That row has no wire. This is a structural violation.
Climber placed outside trellis zone. The support it needs does not exist in that location.
No vertical infrastructure in that row. The crop will sprawl, score will suffer, and the record will reflect it.
You put a climbing crop where there is nothing to climb. That is not creative. That is a penalty.
Hard violation. Climber without trellis. The wire is two rows away and it is not moving.
That crop requires support you are not providing. Placement rejected on structural grounds.
The trellis row exists for exactly this purpose. You chose a different row. I am documenting the decision.
Support infrastructure mismatch. That vine will fall over and the score will follow.
Climber in a ground row. The physics have not changed since last chapter.
That crop needs wire. That cell has dirt. Those are different things.
onion: That one needs something to hold onto and there is nothing there. It is going to fall over.
Wrong row for that one. The wire is right there. Two rows back. You can see it from here.
She built that trellis so the tall ones had a place. This is not that place.
That is a climber sitting on the ground. It is going to do what climbers do without support, which is fail.
I have watched tomatoes fall over in that exact spot. The trellis is right there.
No wire, no support, no chance. Move it back where the infrastructure is.
You are asking that plant to stand up on its own in a row that was never built for standing.
The back row has wire. This row does not. The plant does not know the difference but the score does.
Mom would have caught that before it hit the soil. The climbers go on the wire. Always.
That vine is going to reach for something and find nothing. I know that feeling but the plant should not have to.
vegeman: Ground floor tomato! I tried this once. The results were educational.
No trellis? No problem. The plant will figure it out. The plant will NOT figure it out.
I support this decision in the way that the row does not support that crop.
Watch, it is going to lay down flat and pretend it meant to do that. Tomatoes have no shame.
The wire is literally right there but sure, put the climber in the open row. I admire the confidence.
I did this exact thing in Chapter 2 and everyone looked at me like I suggested arson.
Trellis-free climbing. It is like rock climbing without the rock. Bold.
This is the kind of placement that builds character. In the gardener, not the plant.

gurl: Companion adjacency detected. Score buff active for both cells.
Compatible neighbors. The adjacency report is favorable.
Positive interaction confirmed. Those two crops cooperate and the numbers reflect it.
Companion pairing registered. That is how you turn two average cells into two strong ones.
Adjacency bonus applied. Neighbor compatibility is high.
Good pairing. The biochemistry works even if you did not know the biochemistry.
Mutual benefit confirmed between those two cells. Clean synergy.
Those crops have been growing next to each other for centuries. The bonus is earned.
Compatible adjacency. The bed is cooperating at the cellular level.
Companion match. Score adjusted upward. Continue placing with this level of intention.
onion: Good neighbors. Those two are going to look out for each other all season.
That is not just smart placement. That is dinner planning. Those two belong on the same plate.
She always put the basil next to the tomatoes. Said they grew better together. She was right.
Neighbors that get along. Same in a garden, same on a block. Everything works better.
Those two next to each other is like a conversation that makes both people smarter.
Companion planting. My grandmother called it common sense. The science caught up later.
Good pair. That is the kind of placement that makes the whole row better.
Two crops helping each other grow. That is what a garden is when it is working.
She would put the onions near the carrots and say they kept each other honest. The data backs her up.
That adjacency is going to pay off at harvest. Some neighbors make you better.
vegeman: Companions! Great. But what if you surrounded that tomato with ALL basil? Maximum friendship.
Nice pairing. Now imagine eight of those in a row. An unstoppable flavor army.
They like each other. Cute. I prefer to put enemies next to each other and see who wins.
Companion bonus activated. You could have gotten a bigger bonus if you filled the whole row with companions.
Smart. Boring, but smart. I would have gone with a more aggressive neighbor strategy.
Those two work together. Fine. But have you considered that conflict also builds character?
Companion planting. The safe play. The play that works. The play I never make.
Nice. Those two are going to grow up together and be very well-adjusted plants.

gurl: Conflict adjacency detected. Score penalty active. Those crops do not coexist.
Hostile neighbors. The penalty is applied and will persist through scoring.
Incompatible adjacency. The bed does not negotiate these disagreements. The penalty is automatic.
Conflict pairing. That is a negative interaction that will cost you at harvest.
Those two crops are chemically hostile. The score reflects the hostility.
Adjacency penalty applied. You put enemies next to each other and the bed noticed.
Hostile interaction registered. One of those crops should move. The penalty says which direction.
Conflict confirmed. The adjacency preview showed red for a reason.
Negative pairing. The penalty is modest but cumulative. Stack enough of these and the bed turns on you.
Those crops are allelopathic rivals. That is a polite way to say they are trying to poison each other.
onion: Those two do not get along. You can see it in the leaves before you see it in the score.
Bad neighbors make bad blocks. Same thing in the bed. Move one of those.
She never put those two near each other. Did not explain why. Did not need to.
That is a fight waiting to happen. The roots are already arguing underground.
I have seen that pairing before. It does not end well for either of them.
Two crops that do not like each other sharing a fence. I have seen that on this block too. It never improves.
Conflict adjacency. The kind of thing that costs you in ways you do not notice until harvest.
Those two next to each other is like sitting rival families at the same table. Somebody is leaving early.
Bad pairing. The score is going to hurt but the real cost is what those cells could have been.
She kept the onions away from the beans. Knew they would fight. Forty years of gardens taught her that.
vegeman: Enemies! Right next to each other! This is going to be incredible. Also terrible.
Conflict penalty? More like conflict opportunity. Let them fight. Survival of the fittest.
I love this. Two crops that hate each other, forced to share a border. Reality television for plants.
The preview said red. You placed it anyway. I have never respected anyone more.
Those two are going to have the worst season of their lives and I am here for it.
Hostile adjacency. Also known as my favorite kind of adjacency.
You could move it. Or you could watch nature take its course. I know which one I would pick.
Conflict creates drama. Drama creates interest. Interest creates... actually, it just creates penalties.

gurl: Placement registered.
Cell occupied. Score preview updated.
Crop assigned to cell. Factors are computing.
Noted. The grid is adjusting.
Placement logged. Move on to the next cell when ready.
Cell filled. Twenty-three remaining.
Placement accepted. No violations detected on this cell.
Another cell down. The bed is taking shape.
Registered. The score will tell you if it was wise.
Cell assignment confirmed. Preview is live.
onion: One more in the ground. That is how a garden gets built.
Good. Keep going. The bed fills up one crop at a time.
Something in the dirt. That is always better than nothing in the dirt.
In the ground. That is where it starts. Every time.
Another one placed. The bed is getting fuller.
That is a decision. Every cell is a decision. You are making them.
Placed. Now it belongs to the bed.
One more. The empty spaces are shrinking.
In the soil. Where it does its work. Where everything does its work.
That cell has a crop now. That cell has a future now.
vegeman: Placed. Next. Faster.
One down. Thirty-one to go. Or however many. I am not counting.
Another crop in the dirt. The revolution continues.
Sure. Fine. Put it there. What is the worst that could happen? Do not answer that.
Placed. I would have put it somewhere else but I always say that.
One more soldier in the garden army. Onward.
That works. Probably. I am choosing to believe it works.
Good enough. Perfection is the enemy of having a full bed.

gurl: First placement of the season. The bed is no longer empty. Scoring begins now.
One crop. Thirty-one cells remaining. The record starts here.
First cell occupied. This is the foundation. Make it count or do not. The score will tell the story.
Opening placement registered. The season's data collection has begun.
Cell one. The baseline is set. Everything after this is relative.
First crop in the ground. The bed has been waiting. I have been waiting. Do not make either of us regret it.
Placement one. The empty bed is over. What you build from here defines the season.
Initial crop assigned. The scoring engine is watching.
onion: First one in. That is always the hardest. After this it gets easier. Or harder. Both.
Something in the ground. Finally. An empty bed is the saddest thing in a backyard.
The first crop. She always started in the same corner. Said it felt right to begin where the light hits first.
One in the dirt. The season starts now. Not when the calendar says. When the first crop goes in.
First placement. I am not going to pretend this is not a moment. It is a moment.
You started. That is the part most people never do. The dirt has something in it now.
One crop. One cell. The whole season ahead. Opening day energy.
First one down. The bed looks different with something growing in it. Even just one.
vegeman: ONE crop? Fill the whole bed! What are you waiting for?
First placement. Historic. Boring, but historic.
Finally. I have been watching you stare at this empty bed for what feels like an entire chapter.
One crop in thirty-two cells. At this rate we will be done by winter.
The first crop. The beginning. Now do thirty-one more of those but faster.
You placed one. Congratulations. The bed is three percent full. We are crushing it.
Start small, they said. I disagree but here we are.
One in the ground. Now multiply that feeling by thirty-one and do it immediately.

gurl: Thirty-two of thirty-two. Full bed. Every cell occupied. Now the real scoring begins.
Full capacity. No empty cells. No padding. Every placement decision is load-bearing now.
The bed is full. That is a statement of ambition, not a guarantee of quality.
All cells occupied. Bed density at maximum. The score will determine if density was wise.
Full house. Thirty-two crops. The margin for error is zero because there is nowhere to hide a bad cell.
Complete occupancy. I have seen full beds score lower than half-empty ones. Density is not a virtue without planning.
Every cell filled. The bed is operating at capacity. Whether it is operating well remains to be scored.
Full bed. No buffer cells. No empty padding. This is the layout you committed to.
onion: Every cell. You filled every cell. That is not easy. That is not supposed to be easy.
Full bed. She never filled every cell. Said she liked leaving room for things she had not thought of yet.
Thirty-two crops in thirty-two cells. The bed is full and I am full of feelings about it.
The whole bed. No gaps. That takes planning and nerve in equal measure.
Every cell has something growing. The bed has not been this full since before she stopped planting.
Full. All of it. I need a minute. That is a lot of life in a small space.
You used every square inch. She would have had an opinion about that. Probably a strong one.
Thirty-two crops. The bed is not just a bed anymore. It is a statement.
vegeman: FULL BED. This is what I have been saying since Chapter 2. Fill it ALL.
Every cell. Every single cell. I am vindicated. I am also slightly terrified.
You actually did it. Full bed. I tried this once and it was a disaster but you seem smarter than me.
Thirty-two crops! Maximum chaos! Maximum potential! Maximum chance of something going very wrong!
The bed is full. FULL. No empty space. No breathing room. No regrets. Some regrets.
This is the dream. Every cell occupied. Every cell contributing. Every cell a potential catastrophe.
Full capacity. I feel like a proud parent watching a child make decisions I would have made.
All in. Everything. The whole bed. This is either brilliant or a very organized disaster.

gurl: Average above eight. That is not common. That is earned through placement discipline.
High marks across the board. The bed is performing. I am noting this as a clean season.
Score above threshold by a significant margin. The factors aligned because you aligned them.
Strong result. Sun fit, adjacency, season match. The fundamentals held.
Above eight. The bed is operating at a level I am required to acknowledge as competent.
High score confirmed. No hard violations. No structural waste. Clean work.
Excellent season data. The scoring engine does not give these numbers to beds that guessed their way here.
Top-tier result. The cell average reflects planning, not luck. Luck does not produce consistency.
Score registered above eight point zero. The record shows improvement that is statistically meaningful.
High performance confirmed. I do not give compliments. I give accurate assessments. This one happens to be favorable.
onion: That score. Look at that score. That is what happens when you pay attention to the bed.
Above eight. That is not just passing. That is cooking. That is real food coming out of this bed.
Strong season. The bed gave you everything because you gave it everything first.
I need a minute with this number. That is a real score. That is a score that means something.
High marks. The kind of season where you walk out to the yard and just stand there looking at it.
The bed is producing. Not surviving. Producing. There is a difference and you found it.
That score is the bed saying thank you. Plants do not talk but numbers do.
Above eight. In a Philadelphia backyard. In this soil. With this light. That is something.
Strong result. The kind where you want to call someone and tell them about your garden. Anyone. A stranger.
The bed looks like it knows what it is doing. Because you knew what you were doing.

gurl: Mid-range score. The bed is functional. Functional is not a compliment.
Average in the acceptable range. Not failing. Not excelling. The word you are looking for is adequate.
Score logged. Passing, but not by enough to call it comfortable.
Middle of the range. Some cells carried others. The strong placements masked the weak ones.
Adequate result. The bed did what it could with what you gave it.
Passing score. The violations did not sink you but the missed opportunities are visible in the breakdown.
Mid-range. The bed is performing below its potential, which I know because I scored the potential separately.
Score registered. Room for improvement is a polite way to describe what I see in these numbers.
Not failing. I want to be clear that 'not failing' is where I am setting the bar for this assessment.
The bed survived. The score reflects survival, not ambition.
onion: Passing. That counts. Some seasons you take the pass and move on.
Middle of the road. Not where you want to live but better than the ditch on either side.
Okay. That is an okay season. Okay is not bad. The Phillies have entire months that are just okay.
The bed produced. Not its best, but it produced. That matters.
Mid-range. The kind of season you learn from more than you celebrate.
Passing score. There is food in that number. Not a feast, but food.
Some rows carried. Some rows struggled. That is a season. That is how it goes.
The score is what it is. The bed gave what it could. Next season you give it more to work with.
Not the result you wanted but maybe the result the bed needed. Some seasons teach.
It is a passing grade. I have watched entire Phillies seasons with worse averages.

gurl: Below threshold. The bed underperformed and the data explains why if you care to look.
Low score. The contributing factors are documented in the breakdown. I recommend reading it.
Failing marks. The placements created more problems than the bed could absorb.
Score below five point five. That is not a rounding error. That is a planning deficit.
The bed struggled because the layout asked it to do things it could not do. The score reflects the ask.
Low result. Multiple factor failures compounding. Sun fit alone does not explain this. Nothing alone explains this.
Below threshold. I am required to note this in the season record. The notation is permanent.
Poor performance. The bed is a system. When the inputs are wrong, the system tells you. It told you.
Failing score. The gap between this result and the target is the gap between intention and execution.
Below acceptable range. The bed did not fail. The plan failed. The bed just reported the news.
onion: Rough season. The bed is not angry. It is just tired. Give it something better next time.
Low score. I am not going to pretend that does not sting. It stings.
The bed struggled. You can see it in the leaves. The score just confirms what the leaves already told you.
Hard season. The kind where you sit on the back step and think about what went wrong.
Not the result. Not the one you wanted. But the bed is still there. The soil is still there.
Low marks. The Phillies have had seasons like this. You survive them. You do not celebrate them.
It hurts. A bad score on something you care about hurts. I am not going to pretend otherwise.
The bed gave what it could. It could not give much. That is the score talking.
Rough numbers. But the bed is still standing. The trellis is still up. Next season is still coming.
Bad season. Bad seasons are allowed. What is not allowed is pretending it did not happen.

gurl: Hard violation. Climber without support. The penalty is non-negotiable.
Structural violation detected. The scoring engine applied the full penalty. This was avoidable.
Hard violation on the record. The infrastructure was available. The placement ignored it.
Violation. The kind that costs points and respect simultaneously.
Hard violation logged. The trellis row exists. The climber is not in it. The math is punitive.
Structural infraction. I flag these separately because they indicate a fundamental misunderstanding, not a judgment call.
Violation. The preview warned you. The preview always warns you. The penalty always follows.
Hard violation confirmed. The score takes the hit. The record keeps the notation.
Structural failure in the layout. Not marginal. Not debatable. Wrong.
The violation is clear. A climbing crop was placed without climbing infrastructure. The penalty is applied at full weight.
onion: That one hurts. You can see the plant reaching for something that is not there.
Hard violation. I know what that means in the score. But I also know what it looks like. That plant is struggling.
She would not have made that mistake. But she had forty years. You have had a few chapters.
Wrong spot. The kind of wrong that costs you. Move it next season. The wire is waiting.
Violation. The plant does not know it is a violation. It just knows it has nothing to hold onto.
That is a hard penalty. The kind that drags the whole bed down because one cell is suffering.
Rough. That climber on the ground floor is the saddest thing in this bed right now.
The trellis is right there. Right there. Next season, put the climbers where the wire is.

gurl: Favorable conditions. The bed benefits. Score buff applied to affected cells.
Positive event resolved. The bonus is modest and temporary. Do not restructure around it.
Environmental advantage detected. The affected cells receive a buff through end of season.
Beneficial event. The scoring engine adjusts upward for targeted cells.
Good conditions confirmed. The bonus applies automatically. You did not earn it but you benefit from it.
Positive environmental factor. The bed catches a break. The break is logged.
Favorable event resolution. The buff is applied. The buff is temporary. Plan accordingly.
Conditions in your favor. The score adjusts. Do not mistake luck for strategy.
onion: Good break. The bed needed one and it got one. Take it.
That is a gift from the weather. You take those when they come and you do not ask questions.
Good conditions. The kind of day where you walk out back and everything just looks right.
The bed caught a break. I am choosing to believe we deserved it.
Positive event. Like when the Phillies get a rain delay at the right time. You take the win.
Good news from the garden. That does not happen every season. Appreciate it.
The weather cooperated. The soil cooperated. Everything cooperated. Mark the date.
Favorable conditions. The bed looks grateful. I know how it feels.
critters: Good conditions. We noticed. Moving on.
Favorable. Less to report.
Nothing damaged. Unusual.
Conditions mild. Targets resilient. Unproductive visit.
Environment stable. We will check back when that changes.
Positive event noted. Does not concern us.
Good weather. Bad for our schedule.
Nothing stressed. Nothing vulnerable. Nothing interesting.

gurl: Negative event. Penalty applied to affected cells. The damage is scored.
Adverse conditions. The bed takes a hit. Affected cells are marked in the breakdown.
Environmental threat resolved against you. The penalty persists through harvest scoring.
Negative event on the record. The affected crops are penalized. Intervention was available.
Damage confirmed. The score absorbs it. The question is whether the rest of the bed can carry the loss.
Adverse event. The engine applied the penalty automatically. The engine does not negotiate.
Negative impact logged. Targeted cells took the full modifier. Review the breakdown.
Environmental setback. The penalty is what it is. The season continues with the damage.
onion: That one hurt. The bed took a hit. You can see it in the leaves.
Bad event. The kind where you stand in the yard and just shake your head.
Damage. Real damage. The score is going to show it and the plants are going to feel it.
Rough break. The garden does not always cooperate. Sometimes it punches back.
Negative event. Like a loss in a game that mattered. The standings remember.
That hurt the bed. I can see it from here. The affected cells look different already.
Bad luck. Or bad planning. Or both. The result is the same either way.
The bed got hit. But it is still standing. That is not nothing.
critters: Damage confirmed. Stressed cells identified.
Weakness detected. Exploiting.
Negative event. Affected targets are vulnerable. We are aware.
Stress visible. Stems compromised. Good timing for us.
Damage noted. We did not cause this one. We will benefit from it.
Adverse conditions. Opportunity.
Event resolved. Casualties observed. Standard.
Penalty landed. We are watching what happens next.

gurl: Mixed result. Some cells benefit. Others take damage. Net impact is in the breakdown.
Split event. The modifier is not uniform. Check individual cell reports for actual impact.
Mixed conditions. The bed responds unevenly. That is what happens when diversity meets volatility.
Partial benefit, partial penalty. The math is in the cell-by-cell report.
Mixed event resolved. Some placements held. Some did not. The average will tell the story.
Uneven impact. The crops that matched the conditions gained. The others lost. That is selection pressure.
Mixed result logged. The bed is a system. Systems respond to mixed inputs with mixed outputs.
Split conditions. Winner cells and loser cells. The distinction was made by the event, not by me.
onion: Some good, some bad. That is a season. That is most seasons, actually.
Mixed bag. Part of the bed is fine. Part of it looks rough. Kind of like this block.
Good and bad in the same event. The garden is teaching you that those things coexist.
Half the bed smiled. Half the bed winced. I relate to both halves.
Mixed results. Like a Phillies game where they score six runs and give up seven.
Some cells got lucky. Some did not. The bed does not distribute luck evenly.
Good news and bad news in the same event. I have been to enough Decembers to know that is how it goes.
The bed split. Winners and losers from the same weather. Placement matters more than you think.
critters: Mixed conditions. Some targets stressed. Some not. Partial opportunity.
Uneven damage. We focus on the weak cells.
Split event. Assessing which targets are now viable.
Some crops held. Some did not. We noticed.
Mixed results. Partial vulnerability detected.
Event resolved unevenly. Adjusting approach.
Good for some. Bad for others. Standard.
Damage inconsistent. Targeting the damaged.

gurl: Intervention deployed. Token spent. Modification applied before event resolution.
Action taken. The intervention is on record. Token deducted.
Intervention logged. The modification is active. The event resolves against the modified state.
Token spent. Intervention confirmed. The affected cell is modified.
Action registered. One fewer token this beat. The intervention will show in the breakdown.
Intervention applied. The cell state is updated. Event resolution will account for the change.
Token used. The decision is final. The modification is in effect.
Intervention confirmed. Applied before event. The order of operations is correct.
onion: Good call. Sometimes you have to act before the damage hits.
Intervention used. That is not panic. That is preparation.
You spent the token. The bed is better for it. That is the whole point.
Action taken. The kind of decision that does not feel big but shows up in the score.
Used the intervention. Good. Sitting on your hands when the weather is coming is not a strategy.
Token spent. That cell is protected now. Or swapped. Or mulched. Whatever you chose, you chose.
You intervened. She would have done the same. Watched the weather, moved the row cover, kept going.
Good timing on that. The event was coming and you moved first.

gurl: No intervention. Token forfeited. The event resolves at full effect.
Loss accepted. The bed absorbs the impact without modification.
Intervention declined. The event applies unmitigated. Noted.
Token unused. The damage or benefit resolves as drawn. Your choice.
No action taken. The season continues without intervention. The score absorbs the result.
Intervention skipped. The event lands as designed. Sometimes that is the right call. Sometimes it is not.
Token forfeited. The bed takes whatever the event delivers without buffer.
No intervention deployed. The event resolves cleanly. For better or worse.
onion: No intervention. That takes nerve. Or it takes not caring. I am choosing to believe it was nerve.
You let it ride. Sometimes that is the right move. Sometimes the garden knows better than you do.
Skipped the intervention. The bed takes the full hit. Or the full benefit. Either way, it is raw.
No token used. Okay. The bed will deal with whatever comes. It has dealt with worse.
You chose not to act. That is a decision too. The score will reflect it either way.
Let the event play out. Unfiltered. There is something honest about that even when it hurts.
No intervention. Like watching a Phillies game without changing the channel in the eighth inning.
You held back. Sometimes the best move is no move. Sometimes it is not. We will see at harvest.

gurl: Yield above par. The bed produced what it was designed to produce.
Strong harvest. The yield numbers track with the cell scores. Consistency confirmed.
Good yield across planted cells. The harvest report reflects sound planning.
Production above threshold. The pantry benefits. The record benefits.
Yield confirmed at high level. The crops delivered because the conditions were met.
Harvest yield: above average. The bed converted score into food. That is the point.
Strong production numbers. The yield report is clean. The pantry is stocked.
Good harvest. The bed performed as scored. Score predicts yield. Yield confirms score.
onion: Look at that yield. That is food. Real food from real dirt in a real backyard.
Good harvest. The kind where you bring something inside and put it on the counter and just look at it.
The bed produced. That never gets old. Watching something you planted become something you eat.
Strong yield. That is grocery bags you do not need to carry. That is the garden paying rent.
Good harvest numbers. She would have put these in a basket on the counter. The good basket.
Yield above par. That means the dinner is better. That means the garden mattered today.
Real production from real soil. The numbers are nice but the food is the point.
Strong harvest. The kind you tell the neighbors about. Not to brag. To share.

gurl: Yield below par. The bed underproduced. The factors are in the cell breakdown.
Poor harvest. Production fell short of what the planted cells should have delivered.
Low yield. The gap between potential and actual is documented in the scoring report.
Underperformance at harvest. The pantry receives less than projected.
Yield below threshold. The bed grew what it could with what it was given. It was not given enough.
Poor production confirmed. The harvest report shows the deficit. The causes are traceable.
Low yield numbers. The bed is not underperforming. It is performing exactly as poorly as the layout predicted.
Harvest below par. The pantry feels it. The record shows it. The next season should address it.
onion: Thin harvest. Not much to bring inside. Not much to work with in the kitchen.
Low yield. The bed tried. I am not blaming the bed. I am blaming the season.
Not much came out of that one. Some seasons give. Some seasons teach you what giving costs.
Poor harvest. The kind where you stand in the yard holding three tomatoes and wondering what happened.
Low production. The pantry is light. Next season you plant with the pantry in mind.
Thin yield. She had seasons like this. Said you learn more from the thin ones.
Not enough to cook with. Barely enough to eat standing up in the yard. Which I have done.
Poor harvest. But the bed is still there. The soil is still there. The next season is still coming.

gurl: Harvest inventory matches target recipe. Yield sufficient. Season objective complete.

onion: She always put the basil in the same corner. Row three, column seven. Said it got the afternoon light she liked for drying.
The trellis wire is the same wire she strung. I watched her do it. She used pliers and a level and she did not ask for help.
She grew carrots every fall. Same row. Front row. Said they liked the cool soil near the edge where the concrete breathes.
The tomato seedling came from two doors down. The guy gave it to her over the fence. She grew every tomato in this bed from that line.
She had a spot for the onions. Row two, middle four cells. Said onions liked company but not too much company.
The cedar was from the lumber yard on Oregon Avenue. She picked each board herself. Said you could tell the good cedar by the smell.
She never grew zucchini. Said it took up too much room for what it gave back. I think she was right but I grow it anyway.
The bed sat empty for three years before you pulled the tarp off. Three springs where nothing went in the ground.
She kept a list on the fridge of what went where each season. Pencil on the back of an envelope. Years of data in her handwriting.
She composted in the corner by the fence. Coffee grounds and vegetable scraps. The soil in this bed has decades of that in it.
